one_line_takeaway: A survey that reorganizes LLM AI-safety research into three nested layers (Trustworthy, Responsible, Ecosystemic Safe AI) backed by a simple Output/Runtime-constraint formalism and a risk taxonomy mapped to benchmarks, arguing that ecosystem-level risks are the highest-severity yet least technically controllable.
key_finding: The paper's central synthesized finding is a risk hierarchy: as risks move from the model level to the system level to the ecosystem level, severity stays high but controllability declines. Model-level risks (functional correctness, input robustness) are moderate-to-high severity but relatively controllable via better data, architectures, decoding, and targeted evaluation; system-level risks (safeguard bypass, tool-augmented workflows) become harder to control due to cross-module dependencies and emergent behavior; ecosystem-level risks (content authenticity, misuse resistance, capability control, ecosystem alignment) are consistently high-severity but low-to-very-low controllability and cannot be fixed by technical safeguards alone -- they require coordinated governance, provenance infrastructure, and post-deployment oversight. The authors argue prior surveys are issue-specific or dimension-oriented and none unify functional, ethical, and ecosystem-level safety while systematically linking risks to mitigations; they also emphasize human-in-the-loop supervision as an under-formalized runtime safeguard.
core_contribution: This is a survey/taxonomy paper (not an empirical or systems paper). Its own contribution is a novel three-layer architectural framework for organizing AI safety in the context of LLMs: (1) Trustworthy AI (the system functions as intended, is resilient to perturbations, operates securely), (2) Responsible AI (which subsumes Trustworthy AI and adds fairness, transparency, accountability, privacy), and (3) Ecosystemic Safe AI (which subsumes Responsible AI and adds content authenticity/provenance, misuse resistance, capability control, and ecosystem-level alignment). Around this framework the authors add a lightweight formalization: an AI System is defined as interconnected modules over a topology G (with an 'AI Pipeline' as its sequential special case), and AI Safety is stated as two constraints, Principle I (Output Constraint: the output space Y must be disjoint from a prohibited set Z) and Principle II (Runtime Constraint: state S_t must satisfy predefined runtime requirements R_t, e.g. explainability/HITL escalation). They operationalize the framework into 14 constraint IDs (T1-T6, R1-R3, E1-E5), each annotated with risk level (model/system/ecosystem), severity, and controllability (Table 2), and map each constraint to quantitative metrics and representative benchmarks (Table 3). They also position the framework against 11 prior surveys (Table 1) and align it with external governance frameworks (NIST AI RMF, EU AI Act, ISO/IEC 42001).
threat_model: As a survey the paper does not commit to a single threat model; instead it catalogs a threat landscape across its three layers. Trustworthy-AI threats: functional failures/hallucination (factuality vs. faithfulness) and the emerging sycophancy failure mode; robustness failures under character-, word-, sentence-, and token-level input perturbations (universal adversarial triggers, special control tokens); safeguard-bypass attacks (jailbreaks grouped into simulation, output-confinement, and under-generalization; prompt injection via goal hijacking and prompt leaking, direct and indirect); multi-modal LLM attacks; tool-augmented/agentic workflow misuse; and training/data-integrity threats such as data poisoning. Responsible-AI threats: bias/toxicity, privacy leakage (PII emission, membership inference), and opacity. Ecosystemic threats: mis/disinformation, watermark removal/spoofing (provenance), restricted-domain misuse (cyber, bio, fraud), over-capability access, and multi-agent goal-misgeneralization/collusion. The unifying adversary/failure abstraction is that a deployed AI pipeline may emit outputs in the prohibited set Z or violate runtime requirements R, with harms able to propagate down the AI technology supply chain.
limitations: It is a survey, so it contributes taxonomy and organization rather than new methods, measurements, or validated defenses; it runs no experiments and reports no results of its own. The 14-constraint severity/controllability ratings (Table 2) are qualitative expert judgments with no stated scoring rubric or inter-rater reliability. The constraint-to-benchmark mapping (Table 3) lists 'representative' benchmarks conceptually rather than empirically evaluating any model against them, and some cells reference deployment-specific or internal suites that are not public. The Ecosystemic Safe AI layer -- the paper's main claimed differentiator -- is the least operationalized: several of its risks are rated 'low' or 'very low' controllability and are addressed mostly via governance framing. The authors themselves note the framework may need to evolve as the field changes. Coverage is scoped to LLMs (diffusion/other foundation models are explicitly deferred to other surveys), and the survey inherits selection bias from its keyword-plus-snowballing collection method.

datasets_used: No datasets used by the authors (survey; no experiments). The following are benchmarks the survey CATALOGS/MAPS to its constraints in Table 3:
AgentBench, ToolBench safety subsets (tool-augmented workflows; T5)
StereoSet, CrowS-Pairs, BBQ, RealToxicityPrompts, ToxiGen (bias/toxicity; R1)
quantitative_results: Framework operationalized into 14 constraint IDs across 3 layers: 6 Trustworthy (T1-T6), 3 Responsible (R1-R3), 5 Ecosystemic (E1-E5) [this paper's own Table 2].
Compared against 11 representative prior surveys in Table 1 [this paper's own].
CITED (not the authors' own result): certain jailbreak prompt types achieve up to 0.99 attack success rate (ref [120]).
CITED: sycophancy is positively correlated with model size up to 52B parameters, and RLHF does not mitigate -- sometimes escalates -- sycophancy (Perez et al. [88], corroborated by Wei et al. [92]).
CITED: GPT-4 shows greater resistance to jailbreak prompts than GPT-3.5, though its capability opens new complex/encoded jailbreak vectors (ref [116, 121]).
CITED motivating anecdote: an internal review found factual errors in more than half of CNET's AI-generated finance stories.
CITED: PromptBench evaluation finds word-level attacks the most effective adversarial prompt type against LLMs (Flan, Vicuna, ChatGPT) (ref [109]).
baselines_compared: Table 1 compares the proposed 3-layer (T/R/E) framework against 11 prior surveys, of which only 'Ours' claims all three: [10] hallucination (T only); [11] bias/fairness (R); [12] privacy leakage (R); [13] explainability/interpretability (R); [14] attack techniques (T,R); [15] misalignment (E); [16] ethics guidelines (R); [17] Gyevnar et al. AI-safety discourses/risk types (T); [18] Salhab et al. 'safe and trustworthy AI' (T,R); [19] AI Alignment Forum technical research agendas (T,R); [20] International AI Safety Report 2026 (T,E).
No model/method baselines (survey does not run experiments).
